sop.coo_evaluation:
# SOP Audit & Build Plan – The Forge Belfast

## **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding beyond sign-up**: no documented process for first-class experience, goal-setting conversation, equipment familiarisation, or injury-intake standardisation
- **Retention/win-back mechanics**: no SOP for at-risk member identification, re-engagement campaigns, or lapsed-member reactivation sequences
- **Complaint & incident handling**: no formal process for member complaints, coach conflicts, safety incidents, or escalation paths
- **Health & safety audits**: no documented quarterly/annual H&S review, incident logging, or duty-of-care verification
- **Financial close & reporting**: no SOP for monthly P&L review, revenue reconciliation, or cash-flow forecasting
- **Staffing/payroll**: no process for shift scheduling, time-tracking, payroll verification, or contractor payments
- **Class cancellation protocol**: no SOP for coach absence, last-minute cancellations, or member notification
- **Member data & privacy**: no documented process for GDPR compliance, data retention, or member consent management
- **Email Newsletter** (noted as missing): cadence, approval workflow, content pillars, send mechanics
- **Blog Post** (noted as missing): topic selection, author assignment, SEO brief, publication workflow

## **Needs updating**

- **Client onboarding SOP**: likely assumes friction-free experience; needs real-world first-class checklist, injury-form review, and coach briefing step
- **Billing/subscription SOPs**: may not cover failed payment recovery, refund requests, or pause/freeze mechanics
- **Cancellation/refund policies**: should reference complaint handling—no loop back to improvement
- **Coach induction SOP**: check if it covers studio-specific equipment quirks, member profiling, or The Forge

kpi.advice:
# The Forge – Fractional CFO Actions

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing & AR/M drift is your biggest leak.** AR/M (Average Revenue/Member) has collapsed from £170.43 (December) to £120.48 (June) — a 29% drop despite adding 23 net members. You're either discounting heavily or members aren't taking add-ons. Audit your membership tiers: if most are on entry-level plans, introduce a tiered upsell (nutrition coaching, PT sessions, merchandise) targeting the 147 members. Target: restore AR/M to £160+ by August.

- **Churn spiked to 8.33% in April (relocation disruption) and remains elevated at 5.74% (May) and 0.00% (June shows retention working).** Lock in June's momentum: implement a 90-day onboarding funnel for new members (intro classes, ambassador pairing) to prevent drop-off. Current baseline should be <2%; you're losing £60–80/month per churn member.

- **Quiet class capacity now unused.** You doubled studio size; June shows 147 members but revenue is still flat vs. May (down £2,488). Run a member survey: identify which class times are underbooked, then either consolidate low-attendance slots or launch a corporate/partner gym membership block to fill morning/off-peak slots.

---

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people expenses are your crisis point.** May: £9,233 (46% of revenue). June: £9,390 (53% of revenue). Post-relocation, you've reset baseline costs (rent, utilities, insurance on new space) but they're *not recovering* as revenue grows — this is abnormal. Drill into that £9,390 in June: break down rent vs. supplies vs. utilities. If rent is fixed and higher post-move, you must grow revenue to 60%+ of that cost or renegotiate the lease. Single largest action: get June's supplier/overhead invoices and identify any one-off or inflated line items (e.g. cleaning, equipment maintenance post-fit-out that should now stabilise).

- **Staffing (people costs) is stable and lean** — £3,686 in June on £20k revenue (18.5%) is healthy. No immediate action, but monitor: as members grow to 180+, you may need a second part-time instructor; budget for that *only* after revenue exceeds £22k/month consistently.

---

## IMPROVE PROFITABILITY & TAX

- **Profit % is

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately:** Her Build: Lower (17 sessions) and Build: Upper (9 sessions) should shift to JoJo, who already leads those classes and has capacity. Elevate (6 sessions) and Strongevity (3 sessions) can go to Eilis to develop her breadth and ease JoJo's load.

• **Move Grace's PT and InBody to JoJo and Eilis:** The 11 PT sessions should split: offer 6–7 to Eilis (she's capable and under-loaded at 48 total) and 4–5 to JoJo. Body Composition scans (Grace's 9) are admin tasks—rotate them between all coaches or admin staff, don't count as coaching load.

• **Protect JoJo from overload:** At 133 sessions (44 PT + 109 classes), JoJo is carrying the gym. Even with Grace's class handoff, cap her at ~100 sessions/month by ensuring Eilis takes 15–20 PT sessions and runs 2–3 regular classes solo (e.g., Elevate, Capacity, Plate).

• **Grow Eilis's confidence and PT book:** She's under-utilised at 48 sessions and currently does no PT independently. Give her 15–20 PT slots directly (not shadowing) and encourage her to lead Elevate and Capacity classes solo; this reduces dependency on JoJo and prepares her for future growth.

• **Audit the No Sweat Intro (Grace's 3 sessions):** Clarify whether these are sales/onboarding (not coaching) or mini-classes. If coaching, move to JoJo or Eilis; if admin, remove from coaching KPIs entirely.

• **Schedule Eilis

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. **Add a paid "Open Lift" block on Monday/Thursday afternoons** — your quietest days have dead time between classes. Charge £8–10 for supervised gym access during these windows; appeals to members wanting flexibility without a full class commitment, and monetizes unused capacity.

2. **Run a monthly "Barbell Basics" workshop (Saturday mornings)** — Barbelle is severely underperforming (12 in 30 days). A one-off 45-min technique clinic on a quiet Saturday could rebuild confidence and feed new bodies into the regular class, positioning it as "master the fundamentals before you join the class."

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. **Shift Engine and Capacity to Tuesday/Wednesday mornings and tag them as "train before work"** — your quietest classes are energy-based (conditioning/stamina) but scheduled during low-traffic days. Reposition them at 6:30am on your busiest days when members are already in the gym, and promote them as "quick wins" to the Build class regulars who might layer in a conditioning session.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan for The Forge Service Menu

• **Split into two docs immediately** — one brutally simple for members (pricing, packages, what's in each), one for coaches (upsells, cross-sells, commission structures, how to position each offering). Members get confused by internal logic; coaches need to know *why* to recommend something.

• **Price by outcome, not time** — don't list "1-on-1 session £40/hr." Instead: "8-Week Body Composition Program £320" or "Monthly Strength Foundation Package £150." Members don't buy hours; they buy results. This also anchors higher willingness to pay.

• **Audit what's actually profitable first** — before you finalize anything, calculate: which services have the highest margin? Which ones drive membership retention? Which ones attract new members? If group classes have 80% margin and 1-on-1 has 40%, your menu should *push* group classes harder in the coaching doc.

• **Make the coaches' version a sales tool, not a rulebook** — include: positioning language ("say this"), objection handles ("if she says..., respond with..."), and which packages convert best. If your coaches don't know *how* to sell it, the menu dies in execution.

• **Publish member version by end of week, iterate with coaches next week** — get it live imperfectly rather than waiting for perfection. Watch which questions members ask, adjust the member doc. That data shapes the coaching version.

What's your current offer chaos like — too many add-ons, unclear packages, or just not documented?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

**Steal competitor language (fast).** Spend 2 hours auditing 5-10 women's fitness gyms' landing pages—capture exact phrases they use. Competitors have already tested what converts. Borrow their structure, not their copy.

**Mine your FAQ goldmine.** Pull last 3 months of actual member inquiries (emails, calls, form submissions). The questions women ask = conversion friction. Each FAQ answer becomes a headline or subheading on your page. This is real demand data, not guesswork.

**Test a specific structure:** Hero → "What You Get" (3-4 bullet points with outcomes, not features—"Build confidence in a judgment-free space" beats "women-only cardio classes") → FAQs as collapsible sections → Social proof (before/afters, testimonials) → CTA. This order mirrors buying psychology.

**A/B one element at a time.** Change your main headline only for 2 weeks, measure conversion. Then headline + subheading the next week. Don't rebuild the whole page at once—you won't know what actually moves the needle.

**Add specificity in the "offer" section.** Instead of "fitness classes," list: "Strength training. Pilates. Boxing. Access to female trainers. No male gaze." Women filter by what *won't* be there as much as what will.

**Deadline:** Have the refined page live in 10 days. Measure against current baseline for 30 days.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Feedback System Execution for The Forge

• **Start stupidly small — this week:** One A5 laminated feedback card at the desk asking *one* question only: "What would make you come more often?" Rotate the question monthly. More than one question = 80% non-completion. This isn't a survey, it's a conversation starter you can act on fast.

• **Embed it into existing friction points, not new ones:** Hand it out during class sign-in or water-station time when members are already stopped. Don't create a "feedback station" — people won't go there. Piggyback on moments they're already present.

• **Make acting on feedback visible within 4 weeks:** If someone mentions "childcare during 6pm class is hard," trial a crèche option or pre-class WhatsApp childcare thread *fast*, then tell that member "we did this because you said it." One visible win creates trust and future feedback. Silence kills participation.

• **Digital-only if you must, but make it frictionless:** If using a form, QR code linked to a 60-second Google Form on their phone post-class (not email). But honestly, verbal feedback written down by staff is faster to action and builds relationship. Your gym is small enough for this.

• **Monthly 15-min team huddle (Thursday mornings) to action themes:** Don't hoard feedback. Staff spot patterns faster. If three people mention "music's too loud," the instructor adjusts. If members want a specific class time, you have data to test it.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Reverse-engineer from your actual workflows first**
Don't start with a blank template. Spend 2 hours this week shadowing/recording how YOU currently onboard a membership member and a PT client. Film it. That's your baseline SOP skeleton. Fill gaps from there, not the other way around. Generic SOP templates waste time.

**2. Create a tiered document structure that actually gets used**
- **Page 1**: One-page quick-start (what member sees Day 1)
- **Pages 2-5**: Detailed step-by-step (for your staff/you to deliver consistently)
- **Appendix**: Troubleshooting common questions

Staff will reference page 1. You'll use pages 2-5 to train. This beats a 20-page manual nobody reads.

**3. Task-split by revenue impact and complexity order**
Write SOPs in this sequence: PT onboarding (highest touch, most questions) → Membership → Fuel Forward → Well75 → Strongevity. Each completed SOP reduces admin friction *immediately*. Don't wait to finish all five.

**4. Embed decision trees for your specific service variations**
Example: "Does member have previous gym experience? YES → Skip equipment demo, move to program. NO → Do 30-min floor orientation." Your SOP should handle *your* scenarios, not generic ones.

**5. Assign one accountability owner + schedule 30-min weekly reviews**
You write PT SOP this week. Someone else owns membership next week. Weekly 30-min check = SOPs actually stay current as The Forge evolves, rather than gathering dust.

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Pick ONE platform first (Instagram), batch-create 12 weeks of content in ONE session.** Use Canva templates ($13/mo) to design 3-4 posts in 90 minutes—don't dribble them out. Schedule via Buffer or Later ($5-15/mo). This kills the "what do I post?" paralysis that kills consistency.

• **Steal your best-performing content themes NOW.** Pull your last 3 months of posts. Which got most engagement? (Transformation stories? Class schedule? Member spotlights?) Build your calendar around *those* patterns, not generic fitness content. If member wins get 3x engagement, make those 50% of your posts.

• **Create a repeating content mix that requires minimal thinking:** Mon = Member spotlight (repost tagged member), Wed = Class highlight or strength tip (film during existing sessions—no extra shoot), Fri = Community/weekend vibe (low-effort). This removes decision-making and ensures you're posting what converts.

• **Block 90 minutes every OTHER Sunday to batch-film 8-10 quick videos** (30 seconds each) during actual classes. Phone tripod + natural lighting. This feeds Instagram Reels, which Instagram's algorithm heavily favors over static posts—drive real reach without more posting frequency.

• **Build a 3-line "post-to-DM" playbook:** When a post hits (engagement or comments), you personally comment on 3-5 comments within 24 hours with a question. This isn't content management—it's direct customer conversations that convert DMs into bookings/trials.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Executing Videography Sessions with Jordan

**Lock it in NOW, not later**
- Book all 8-week slots for the next 12 months TODAY (6 sessions minimum). This removes the friction of repeated scheduling and ensures Jordan actually blocks the dates. Grace won't "get around to it" if it's already calendared.

**Define the output before the first shoot**
- Agree with Jordan upfront: X reels for Instagram, Y testimonial clips, Z facility/class footage per session. Get a delivery checklist in writing. This prevents vague "let's see what we get" shoots and wasted time in post-production.

**Shoot during peak class times (but off-peak for distraction)**
- Schedule shoots 15 minutes *before* or *after* key classes—high energy, real members visible in background. Avoid empty gym sessions; avoid disrupting live classes. This gets authentic footage AND reduces scheduling conflicts.

**Create a simple pre-shoot brief**
- One week before each session, send Jordan a single-page brief: This month focus = "member transformations" or "strength progressions" or "community vibes." Tells him what to prioritize without micromanaging. Keeps content aligned to current marketing themes.

**Batch-repurpose ruthlessly**
- Don't sit on footage. Within 2 weeks of each shoot, have Jordan deliver edited clips ready to post. Grace schedules posts in batches (e.g., 3 reels released across the following month). This extends ROI of each session across 4-6 weeks of content.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries — The Forge

**The Real Problem You're Solving:** Trial enquiries are likely sitting in your inbox or CRM with inconsistent follow-up timing, causing drop-off before conversion.

**Here's what to actually build:**

• **Set up a 3-touch automated sequence in your CRM/email tool (Mailchimp, HubSpot, Zapier):** Day 0 (enquiry received) → welcome + trial booking link, Day 3 → social proof + "spots filling fast" nudge, Day 7 → final offer + urgency ("trial ends Friday"). Test this for 2 weeks, measure booking conversion %, then optimize messaging.

• **Automate the admin gate:** Enquiry form submission → auto-creates WhatsApp/SMS confirmation with trial start date & class schedule link + payment link. Remove the manual "someone reply to them" step entirely. Grace shouldn't touch this.

• **Track one metric ruthlessly:** trial-to-paid conversion rate. Currently what % of enquiries become paying members? Set a target (e.g., 35% → 50%), measure after 3 weeks. If it's not moving, the bottleneck isn't the automation—it's your onboarding experience or trial classes themselves.

• **Front-load the booking friction:** Make the trial booking link the easiest path. One click = time slot selected + payment captured. No "we'll call you back" nonsense. You'll know real intent immediately.

**Action today:** Audit your last 20 trial enquiries. How long from enquiry to first touch? How many converted? That's your baseline.

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Role-Based Weekly Checklists for The Forge

• **Start with outcome backwards** — For each person, define 2-3 non-negotiable KPIs they directly control (e.g., JoJo: member retention rate + class attendance; Eilis: new enquiries converted; Sarah: revenue per class/product sold). Build the checklist around *only* what moves those metrics, not busywork.

• **Make it scannable & ownable** — Use a simple one-pager per role with Monday priorities, mid-week checkpoint, Friday review. Include the actual KPI number they're tracking (not "increase sales" but "£X revenue target for the week"). People execute when they see their specific number.

• **Build in Friday 10-min syncs** — Each person reports their 3 KPIs in a quick huddle. This isn't about perfection; it's about catching blockages early (JoJo stuck on retention? Eilis waiting on marketing assets?). Use this to adjust next week's checklist.

• **Embed one "growth task" per role weekly** — Don't just firefight. Each person gets one micro-project (JoJo: test new retention email; Eilis: follow up 5 stalled prospects; Sarah: trial a new class upsell script). Rotate these so you're building momentum, not just maintaining.

• **Version it in week one, then lock it** — Trial the checklists for 2 weeks, tweak based on real bottlenecks, then treat it as your operational standard. Make it simple enough that absence doesn't break it.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Vision Session Execution for The Forge

**Before you meet (do this solo first):**
- Write down the ONE sentence that finishes: "In 90 days, The Forge will be known for ___" (e.g., "the strongest community retention rate in Belfast"). This is your north star — frame everything against it.

**The 90-minute session structure:**
- **15 min:** Show your SSO (Shared Sense of Outcome) — literally display it. Not a presentation, a conversation starter. Ask: "Does this feel right to you?" Get real pushback now.
- **30 min:** Break down your 90-day plan by *outcome*, not task. "We need 12 new retention-focused habits implemented across the gym" — then show the 3 priority streams (e.g., onboarding redesign, community events, referral system). Don't hide the hard bits.
- **30 min:** Define each person's **single primary lane** with a specific KPI they own (e.g., "Sarah owns member experience — success = 85% retention rate"; "Tom owns operations — success = 4 failed bookings max per month"). Clarity kills politics.
- **5 min close:** "Who needs clarity before you walk out?" Don't let ambiguity live in the room.

**Critical move:** Record it on your phone and send the clip to them after. People forget. Written + watched = remembered.

What's your current team size?

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalancing — The Forge (30 days)

**1. Map reality, not roles**
Stop at job titles. For one week, have each team member log actual time spent on: classes taught, admin, member queries, cleaning/maintenance, social media, sales conversations. Use a simple Google Sheet or Toggl. You'll find gym owners severely underestimate what pulls staff away from their core job. Grace — you're probably doing 4 jobs yourself.

**2. Identify the bottleneck person (usually you)**
Run the numbers. If Grace is teaching 12 classes/week + handling member complaints + managing social + chasing late payments, she's your constraint. Rebalancing fails if you don't move something off yourself first. Pick ONE admin/operational task to fully hand over by day 10 (e.g., weekly schedule planning or member onboarding calls).

**3. Redistribute by impact, not equality**
Don't aim for "fair hours." Redistribute by what breaks member experience if it's neglected. If member retention drops when classes aren't promoted, social media moves up. If no one answers emails within 6 hours, reception coverage is the priority. Align workload to what actually drives revenue.

**4. Trial + measure (days 15-30)**
Implement the new split for 2 weeks, then check: Are classes full? Are member complaint emails answered within 4 hours? Is staff morale higher? Adjust week 3-4 based on actual friction, not assumptions. One person will still be overloaded — move something else.

**What's the current biggest bottleneck at The Forge — Grace, a specific staff member, or a

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Audit your big three first (they'll drive 80% of savings):**
- **Rent/lease**: Call your landlord with 6 months' data showing membership trends. Negotiate a reduced rate NOW in exchange for a 2-year commitment, or explore relocating to a cheaper Belfast location (Eastside/Southside often 15-20% cheaper than city centre). Even 1-2% here = thousands saved.

**Staffing smart (without cutting service):**
- Shift your PT model: Move 1-2 salaried coaches to commission-only or hybrid (base + commission). Saves 20-30% on that line item if done carefully. Replace one full-time admin role with 2x part-time staff—you get coverage flexibility + lower NI contributions.

**Utilities + supplier squeeze:**
- Contact your energy provider NOW with last 12 months' bills. Get a competitive quote from a business broker (takes 20 mins). Water heating gyms often = biggest waste—install motion sensors on changing room lights, adjust thermostat 1°C lower (members won't notice; you save 5-8%).
- Renegotiate equipment maintenance contracts. You're likely overpaying—ring 2-3 competitors for quotes, mention you'll switch.

**The quick win (do this week):**
- Audit your software stack. You're probably paying for apps nobody uses (forgotten Zen or Mindbody add-ons, old marketing tools). Cut them. Chase down any unused corporate memberships you're subsidizing.

**Track it obsessively:** Create a simple spreadsheet tracking savings by category weekly.

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Execution Plan for Grace

**Get the right data source locked down first**
- Don't manually hunt through bank statements. Set up a direct integration (Xero/QuickBooks to a simple Google Sheet or dashboard) so figures auto-pull daily. Grace's time is worth more than data entry.

**Pick 3-4 metrics that actually drive decisions at The Forge**
- Don't track everything. Focus on: (1) Monthly recurring revenue by membership tier, (2) cash runway (expenses vs. reserve), (3) cost-per-acquisition per marketing channel, (4) member lifetime value. These tell Grace whether to invest in ads, cut costs, or adjust pricing — not vanity numbers.

**Set a fixed 90-minute monthly review slot (same day each month)**
- Schedule it for the 3rd working day after month-end so data settles. Grace reviews for 60 mins, flags one decision/action (e.g., "pivot ad spend to Instagram"), and communicates it to the team in 30 mins. Consistency beats perfection.

**Link KPIs to one tactical decision per month**
- Example: If member acquisition cost rises 15% month-on-month, that *automatically* triggers a review of which ad platform to cut. Make the metrics force action, not just awareness.

**Delegate the dashboard refresh (don't do it yourself)**
- Train a part-time admin or gym manager to pull/update the sheet. Grace only interprets and decides. This scales when you grow.

What's her biggest blocker right now — data chaos, unclear metrics, or analysis paralysis?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Pull last 12 months of bank & membership data TODAY.** Export transactions, membership sign-ups/cancellations, and class attendance by month. You need the raw numbers before you can forecast anything. Spend 2 hours max organizing this into a spreadsheet — messy is fine, complete matters.

• **Model three scenarios: Conservative (10% growth), Base (25%), Aggressive (40%).** For each, calculate: monthly membership revenue, class pack sales, PT sessions, and fixed costs (rent, utilities, staff). This shows Grace what she's actually chasing and where profit sensitivity lives. Most gym owners guess — you'll have data.

• **Identify your ONE cash-flow pinch point.** Is it seasonal (summer slump? January surge?). Is it payroll timing vs. membership billing cycles? Build a 13-week rolling cash forecast to flag when you'd need a buffer. This prevents panic decisions later.

• **Set a simple monthly tracking dashboard.** 5 KPIs max: membership count, churn rate, revenue vs. forecast, class utilization %, operating margin. Review every Friday for 15 minutes. This catches trends before they become problems — and gives you decision data for marketing/pricing moves.

• **Use this to make ONE decision this week.** Does the forecast justify hiring that extra instructor? Can you afford a discount campaign to hit your base case? Forecasting only matters if it drives action.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Building Your Service P&L — The Forge Edition

• **Start with your three revenue buckets separately**: Pull last 3 months of class fees (group revenue ÷ class count = revenue per class), PT session fees, and add-on sales (smoothies, merchandise, etc.). Don't overthink it—spreadsheet with dates, amounts, service type.

• **Cost-track ruthlessly by service type**: Classes = instructor pay + facility overhead split by class attendance; PT = trainer commission + minimal overhead; Add-ons = COGS only (wholesale cost of smoothie ingredients, etc.). For overhead, allocate based on time/space used—PT takes studio 1:1, classes are batched.

• **Spot your margin winners fast**: PT likely sits at 60-70% margin (once booked, minimal incremental cost). Classes probably 40-50% after instructor pay. Add-ons could be 50%+ if you're not overbuying stock. This ranking tells you where to push effort immediately.

• **Stress-test with your real numbers**: If you run 12 classes/week at £8/head with average 8 women attending, that's £768/week revenue. If your instructor costs £60/class, you're at ~£500/week profit per class stream. Compare that to PT: if you do 20 PT sessions/month at £40 take-home per session, that's £800/month—slower growth but higher margin per transaction.

• **Use this to decide next month's focus**: Once you see the gaps, you'll know whether to fill empty PT slots, raise class prices, or stock better add-ons. Build it this week; decide by Friday.

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan: Automation Audit for The Forge

• **Map it visually first (30 mins):** Pull every active automation from your platform (Zapier/Make/native CRM) and list them in a spreadsheet by trigger → action. You'll spot duplicates immediately—e.g., "new enquiry" firing the same welcome email twice through different paths. This is your audit foundation.

• **Test the critical path manually:** Pick 5 test email addresses and submit enquiries through your website form yourself. Walk the *actual* journey: Does the welcome email land? Does the trial booking link work? Does the follow-up sequence fire at day 2, 5, 7? Don't assume it works—verify every step because most gyms have broken automations they never catch.

• **Kill duplicates ruthlessly:** If two automations do the same job, delete the older one immediately. Common culprit at gyms: duplicate "trial booking confirmation" emails triggering from both the form AND the booking platform. Choose one owner, delete the other.

• **Check your drop-off points:** Where do enquiries get stuck? (Trial link expired? Payment gateway failing? SMS not sending?) Run a report on last 50 enquiries—how many actually completed? That gap is your real problem to solve next.

• **Lock in the sequence timing:** Ensure it's actually *moving* people: day 0 (welcome), day 1 (trial reminder), day 3 (didn't show? re-engage), day 7 (convert or nurture). Sloppy timing = dead leads.

**Do this this week.** You'll likely uncover 2-3 broken revenue steps.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — Concrete Execution

**1. Identify your 3 tasks FIRST (the right ones matter)**
— Scan last week: What did YOU or your team do 3+ times identically? Likely candidates: member onboarding emails + form responses, class booking confirmations + schedule syncs, or social media content resharing across platforms. Pick tasks that consume >5 hours/week combined — anything less isn't worth the setup friction.

**2. Start with the messiest repetitive task, not the easiest**
— Build automation for your **member inquiry → onboarding sequence** first. Every new member inquiry probably gets a manual reply, a form sent, a follow-up chase, and a manual schedule entry. Use Zapier or Make to trigger a welcome email → form → calendar add automatically. This alone saves 3-4 hours/week and compounds (more members = more automation wins).

**3. For the other 2, target "copy-paste" work**
— **Class schedule posts**: Build a simple template in Canva, then use Buffer/Later to auto-post to Instagram, Facebook, TikTok simultaneously. **Payment/cancellation notifications**: Set your gym management software (ClassPass, Mariana Tek, etc.) to auto-send confirmations — most already have this buried in settings; you just need to activate.

**4. Don't automate perfectly — automate fast**
— Your first automation will feel rough. That's fine. Build it in 2 hours max using existing tools (Zapier templates, not custom coding), test with 5 members, tweak. Perfectionism kills momentum here.

**What's Grace's actual bottleneck —

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website - Execution Plan

• **Quick wins first (this week):** Install SSL certificate (forces HTTPS—non-negotiable for payment pages), enable two-factor authentication on your WordPress/CMS admin panel, change all default passwords. Cost: £0-50. Time: 2 hours. This blocks 80% of amateur attacks while you plan bigger moves.

• **Get a security audit, not a consultant:** Pay for one targeted penetration test (£300-600, 2-3 hours) from a firm that specializes in small gyms/SaaS. Tells you exactly what's broken. Don't hire someone on retainer yet—you need to know if you're actually at risk first.

• **Member data is your liability:** If you're storing payment info or health data, you're legally exposed. Either use Stripe/PayPal for payments (they hold PCI compliance, not you) or hire a freelancer to audit your database immediately. One breach = reputation death + potential fines.

• **Assign one person ownership:** Pick someone on your team (or yourself) to own this. Monthly password rotation, plugin updates, backup checks. It's not a project—it's an ongoing responsibility. Set calendar reminders.

• **Build this into your marketing:** Once secure, mention it. "Your privacy matters—[gym name] uses enterprise-grade security" converts nervous online bookers and differentiates you from competitors.

What's your current payment setup?

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

1. **Lead quality and volume have collapsed.** You've gone from 69 leads in April (£6/lead) to 2 in July (£55/lead). This isn't a gradual decline—it's a cliff. The funnel is broken, not just inefficient. Your close rate has also tanked: April-June averaged 50%+ conversion; July is 0%.

2. **July's spend drop reveals the real problem.** You cut spend to £109 (down 74% from April), which suggests you or your team already noticed trouble and throttled back. But throttling without fixing the underlying issue just delays the reckoning. The £55 CPL in July isn't cheaper; it's a symptom of dead creative, poor targeting, or audience fatigue.

3. **Something material changed between June and July.** Seasonal drop, creative rotation, audience saturation, iOS privacy changes hitting targeting, or a shift in who you're reaching. The business fundamentals (trial offer, gym quality) almost certainly didn't—your April performance proves the offer converts. The ads themselves or their reach did.

---

**Actions**

1. **Audit and refresh creative immediately.** Pull the exact ads running in July vs June. Check: are they the same visuals/copy? If yes, you're hitting a burnt-out audience. Test 3–5 new creative angles: before/after transformations, member testimonials (video), class-specific content (HIIT/strength focus), pain-point messaging ("tired of mixed gyms?"), and local community angle. Launch these at £50/day budget to stress-test fast.

2. **Tighten targeting and test new audiences.** Your April 69 leads came from somewhere—find that audience definition (age, interests, location radius, lookalike source) and reactivate it. July's 2 leads suggests you're either showing ads to the wrong people or the right people are seeing dead creative. Test: narrow geographic radius (Belfast postcode only if you haven't), exclude past engagers (they've tuned out), and create a fresh lookalike off your April-June sales (not just leads).

3. **Restore spend gradually behind winning creative only.** Don't jump back
